startScreenOptions.title: Pons — Interactive Lecture
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 5 embedded questions for AN59.1-AN59.4.

interactions.label: In locked-in syndrome caused by pontine haemorrhage, vertical eye movements are

params.question: In locked-in syndrome caused by pontine haemorrhage, vertical eye movements are preserved while horizontal gaze is lost. Which anatomical explanation best accounts for this difference?

answers.text: The abducens nucleus is inherently resistant to ischaemic damage

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The abducens nucleus is not selectively resistant. It is destroyed along with the basis pontis. The key is that vertical gaze centres are located above the lesion, not that horizontal gaze centres are more vulnerable.

answers.text: Vertical gaze is controlled by midbrain structures located above the level of the pontine lesion

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The midbrain houses the rostral interstitial nucleus of the MLF and other structures governing vertical gaze. Since the midbrain sits above the pons, it escapes the haemorrhage and vertical eye movements survive.

answers.text: Vertical gaze pathways descend through the spinal cord, bypassing the pons entirely

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Vertical gaze pathways do not travel through the spinal cord. They originate in the midbrain. The preservation of vertical gaze is due to the midbrain being above the pontine lesion, not because the pathways bypass the pons via a different route.

answers.text: The paramedian pontine reticular formation controls both vertical and horizontal gaze, but vertical fibres recover faster

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The PPRF is the horizontal gaze centre only. Vertical gaze is controlled by separate midbrain structures. The distinction is anatomical location, not speed of recovery.

overallFeedback.feedback: The key principle is anatomical localisation. Horizontal gaze depends on the PPRF in the pons, which is destroyed. Vertical gaze depends on midbrain structures above the lesion, which are spared. Location of the centre, not resilience of the fibres, determines what survives.

Well done. Locked-in syndrome spares vertical gaze because the midbrain, which controls it, lies above the pontine haemorrhage. This is a classic example of how anatomical localisation predicts which functions survive a lesion.

metadata.title: In locked-in syndrome caused by pontine

interactions.label: On the posterior surface of the pons, the sulcus limitans is a longitudinal groo

params.question: On the posterior surface of the pons, the sulcus limitans is a longitudinal groove on the floor of the fourth ventricle. What does it separate?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The basis pontis and tegmentum are ventral and dorsal divisions seen on cross-section, not features visible on the posterior surface. The sulcus limitans is a surface landmark on the ventricular floor with a different organisational role.

answers.text: The upper pons from the lower pons

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The upper and lower pons are distinguished by their internal contents (e.g. trigeminal nuclei above, abducens and facial nuclei below), not by a single longitudinal groove. The sulcus limitans runs along the length of the ventricular floor.

answers.text: Medial efferent areas from lateral afferent areas

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. The sulcus limitans is a key embryological and functional boundary. Motor (efferent) nuclei develop medially, while sensory (afferent) nuclei develop laterally. This groove preserves that organisation on the ventricular floor.

answers.text: The fourth ventricle from the cerebral aqueduct

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The transition from the fourth ventricle to the cerebral aqueduct occurs at the junction of the pons and midbrain. The sulcus limitans is a groove within the ventricular floor itself, not a boundary between cavities.

overallFeedback.feedback: The sulcus limitans separates medial efferent (motor) areas from lateral afferent (sensory) areas on the floor of the fourth ventricle. This reflects the embryological basal plate–alar plate organisation that persists in the adult brainstem.

Exactly right. The sulcus limitans reflects the embryological separation of the basal plate (motor) medially from the alar plate (sensory) laterally — a principle that applies throughout the brainstem.

interactions.label: In the lower pons, the pontine nuclei are located in the dorsal tegmentum alongs

params.question: In the lower pons, the pontine nuclei are located in the dorsal tegmentum alongside the cranial nerve nuclei.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: The pontine nuclei are actually in the ventral basis pontis, interspersed among the descending fibre bundles. Their job is to relay cortical motor commands to the cerebellum. The dorsal tegmentum houses the cranial nerve nuclei — the abducens nucleus (CN VI) near the midline and the facial motor nucleus (CN VII) ventrolateral to it. Keeping this ventral–dorsal separation clear is essential for reading pontine cross-sections.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct — the pontine nuclei are in the ventral basis pontis, not the dorsal tegmentum. They are grey matter clusters scattered among the descending corticospinal and corticobulbar fibres, relaying cortical signals to the cerebellum via the transverse pontocerebellar fibres. The cranial nerve nuclei (CN VI and CN VII) occupy the dorsal tegmentum.

interactions.label: The facial nerve (CN VII) has multiple functional components in the pons. Which

params.question: The facial nerve (CN VII) has multiple functional components in the pons. Which nucleus provides parasympathetic innervation to the submandibular, sublingual, and lacrimal glands?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: The facial motor nucleus supplies the muscles of facial expression — it is a somatic motor component. Parasympathetic secretomotor fibres to the glands arise from a different nucleus.
The nucleus tractus solitarius receives taste afferents from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue via the chorda tympani. It is a sensory relay, not a parasympathetic motor nucleus.

Correct. The superior salivatory nucleus provides parasympathetic preganglionic fibres that travel via the chorda tympani to the submandibular and sublingual glands, and via the greater petrosal nerve to the lacrimal gland.
The spinal trigeminal nucleus processes pain and temperature from the face. It handles a small area of somatic sensation around the ear for CN VII, but has no parasympathetic function.

overallFeedback.feedback: The superior salivatory nucleus is the parasympathetic component of CN VII. The facial nerve has four components: the motor nucleus (facial expression), superior salivatory nucleus (glandular secretion), nucleus tractus solitarius (taste), and spinal trigeminal nucleus (ear sensation). Each component can be affected independently by lesions at different levels.

Well done. Remembering the four functional components of CN VII — motor, parasympathetic (superior salivatory), taste (NTS), and somatic sensory (spinal trigeminal) — is essential for understanding facial nerve lesions at different levels.

metadata.title: The facial nerve (CN VII) has multiple f

interactions.label: What clinical feature distinguishes Millard-Gubler syndrome from Raymond syndrom

params.question: What clinical feature distinguishes Millard-Gubler syndrome from Raymond syndrome?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Both Millard-Gubler and Raymond syndrome include contralateral hemiplegia from corticospinal tract involvement. This feature is shared, not distinguishing.
Both syndromes produce ipsilateral lateral rectus palsy from involvement of the abducens nerve (CN VI). This is a common feature, not a differentiating one.

answers.text: Contralateral loss of fine touch and proprioception

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Contralateral sensory loss from medial lemniscus involvement is a feature of Foville syndrome, not Millard-Gubler or Raymond syndrome. Neither of these two syndromes includes sensory loss.

Correct. Both syndromes share CN VI palsy and contralateral hemiplegia. Millard-Gubler adds damage to CN VII fascicles, producing ipsilateral LMN facial palsy. Raymond syndrome spares CN VII entirely. This is the defining difference between the two.

overallFeedback.feedback: Raymond syndrome has two features: ipsilateral CN VI palsy and contralateral hemiplegia. Millard-Gubler syndrome adds a third: ipsilateral lower motor neuron facial palsy from CN VII fascicle damage. That facial palsy is the key distinguishing feature. Foville syndrome goes further still, adding medial lemniscus involvement (contralateral sensory loss) and horizontal gaze palsy.

Exactly. Raymond syndrome involves CN VI and the corticospinal tract only. Millard-Gubler adds CN VII fascicle damage, producing an ipsilateral LMN facial palsy. This makes Millard-Gubler the more common and more clinically apparent of the two presentations.

summary: Review the video interactions to check your understanding.
